retrieved_docs.title: Opsoclonus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Opsoclonus differs from opsochoria, which involves dysconjugate eye movements, as well as ocular flutter, which is restricted to the horizontal plane. Unlike nystagmus, the phase moving the eye away from the target is always a saccade. When opsoclonus occurs with myoclonus or ataxia, encephalopathy, generalized tremor, or impaired cognition and behavioral changes, the presentation is classified as opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome (OMS), also known as dancing eye and dancing feet syndrome. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Opsoclonus -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Recognizing symptoms of OMS is essential for early detection. Patients and caregivers should be alert to rapid, uncontrolled eye movements (opsoclonus), ataxia, and myoclonic jerks. Unexplained movement disorders, developmental regression, or suspected paraneoplastic symptoms warrant urgent neurology and oncology evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Kuru -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Ambulatory phase : Presents with features of cerebellar ataxia. There is a subtle gait unsteadiness that progresses to ataxia and incoordination of the muscles of the trunk and the lower limbs. Shivering of the body or "kuru," which is exacerbated by lower temperatures, is present. Titubation and other abnormal movements of the body are presenting signs. Curling of feet and clawing of toes are observable for maintaining a balance. Patellar clonus and ankle clonus are the hallmark clinical picture. As the disease progresses, the Romberg sign may be present. Cerebellar signs such as nystagmus, dysarthria, dysmetria, and intention tremor are also reported. Individuals are also emotionally labile and may exhibit sporadic uncontrolled laughter. Hence the disease is also referred to as the "laughing disease."

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonus -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Myoclonus can be the cause of significant disability, and impairments can include problems with gait, activities of daily living, and possibly the emergence of intolerable frustration, which can, in turn, prompt depressive symptoms. As noted, myoclonus is often treatment-refractory, in some cases even to polypharmaceutic approaches, and this naturally exacerbates the frustrating nature of living with myoclonus. Occupational therapy can assist with the use of assistive devices to help manage myoclonic movements with large excursions. Similarly, physical therapy can help instruct the correct and safe way to employ ambulatory assistive devices.

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonus -- Etiology -- Neurodegenerative
retrieved_docs.content: Parkinson disease (PD) Myoclonus occurs much less commonly in idiopathic PD than the more pathognomonic and easily recognized high-amplitude, low-frequency, and usually asymmetric rest tremor. That said, rest tremors seem to occur less frequently in those PD patients who have concomitant myoclonus. In PD patients with myoclonus, the cortical burden of aberrant α-synuclein is markedly higher than in those who do not exhibit myoclonus. In the mid-1970s, myoclonus in Parkinson disease was understood as an adverse effect of therapy with levodopa. [19] Bromocriptine and amantadine have also been associated with myoclonus as an adverse effect, although pharmacological databases (eg, Epocrates) no longer list this effect. Myoclonus is recognized more as an “off” phenomenon in PD according to current understanding. Orthostatic myoclonus of the lower extremities can exacerbate unsteadiness in PD. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Morvan Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the central nervous system, hyperexcitability manifests via confusion, behavioral changes, myoclonus, and severe insomnia; hallucinosis and encephalopathy may also occur. Autonomic hyperactivity includes hyperhidrosis, constipation, labile blood pressure, hemodynamic instability, and cardiac arrhythmias. Finally, peripheral nervous system hyperexcitability is typically demonstrated by painful muscle cramps, myokymia (vermicular or continuous rippling movements, often encountered as eyelid twitching), and neuromyotonia (involuntary continuous muscle fiber activity, often seen as fasciculations, doublet and triplet discharges on electromyography). [3] In short, MoS combines peripheral and central nervous system hyperexcitable states; myokymia, neuromyotonia, muscle fasciculations, stiffness, autonomic nervous dysfunction, severe insomnia, and encephalopathy are commonly encountered, though most patients manifest only some of the symptoms that may occur.

retrieved_docs.title: High-Pressure Neurological Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Tremor is the most characteristic symptom of HPNS, occurring both at rest and during movement. Tremor typically begins in the distal extremities and may progress to involve the entire body. The frequency of these tremors ranges from 8 to 12 Hz. [8] The amplitude increases with faster compression speed and increasing hydrostatic pressure. In addition, spontaneous (constant) eye oscillations in random directions, known as opsoclonus, are among the earliest signs of HPNS. [2] Other possible symptoms of HPNS include headache, dizziness, fatigue, myoclonic jerking, muscular weakness, and euphoria. [3] [8] [9] [34] [35] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Shy-Drager Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Motor dysfunction, dysautonomia, and sleep disorders form the backbone of the history and physical exam findings present in patients with MSA. Parkinsonism features are predominated in MSA-P and are characterized by physical exam findings that include postural instability, tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia. [8] Dysautonomia occurs in both MSA-P and MSA-C in the form of urinary dysfunction, anhydrosis, and orthostatic hypotension. [5] Disturbances in sleep include sleep apnea, stridor, and ataxic breathing, which also occur with MSA. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Fahr Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Clinical Features
retrieved_docs.content: (A) Movement disorder like features: [4] Signs and symptoms resembling parkinsonism, such as bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor, hypophonia, hypomimia, mask-like facies, shuffling gait Clumsiness Fatigability Gait dysfunction Choreoathetosis Dystonia Slurred speech Muscle cramping Pyramidal or cerebellar symptoms (rarely) (B) Neuropsychiatric features [5] Depression Apoplexia Dementia, mostly fronto-executive type resembling subcortical dementia in Wilson disease and Huntington disease Concentration deficits Behavioral changes (C) Other CNS features [6] [3] Loss of consciousness Tetany Seizures Spasticity Speech impairment Myoclonus Coma Papilledema Chronic headache Vertigo Urinary urgency or incontinence Impotence Severe hypertension

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonus -- Etiology -- Neurodegenerative
retrieved_docs.content: Progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP) Motoric deficits in PSP can occur anywhere along the neuraxis. Several discrete phenotypes have been described, [23] centering on bradykinesia, rigidity, gait freezing and falls, supranuclear ocular motor impairment, dysphagia, dysarthria, incontinence, an array of behavioral changes, including frontal cognitive dysfunction, and sleep disorders. [24] Myoclonus is uncommon in the classical PSP phenotype, known as Richardson’s syndrome, in which symmetrical axial rigidity is accompanied by vertical gaze palsy, severe akinesia, and falls. Myoclonus does occur in the combined PSP-CBS phenotype, which features prominent asymmetric dystonia, cortical sensory deficits, and the alien limb phenomena. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Decorticate Posturing -- Clinical Significance -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Paratonia, or gegenhalten, appears in patients with encephalopathy or neurodegenerative disorders. Spasticity involves increased muscle tone with preserved consciousness. Cerebellar fits produce opisthotonic and decerebrate-like posturing, often accompanied by drop attacks without altered awareness. [13] Paroxysmal autonomic instability with dystonia presents as episodic dystonia with hemihidrosis, tachycardia, tachypnea, and mydriasis. [14] [15] Tonic status epilepticus may also mimic posturing patterns in unresponsive patients. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonus -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The logic underlying the diagnostic approach should be carefully and thoroughly explained to the patient. The clinician should also review the rationale underlying the eventual treatment plan with the patient, including a discussion of possible adverse effects and strategies to escalate pharmacotherapy if the initial approach is insufficient. Finally, the clinician should ensure that the patient understands the vital role that other support plays in treatment success: the key here is accessing all services to improve the holistic dimensions of the care plan, including psychiatric care for co-occurring depression or anxiety, along with physical and occupational therapy, all in the service of optimizing outcomes and quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonus -- Etiology -- Essential myoclonus
retrieved_docs.content: The degree of dysfunction caused by essential myoclonus varies, and for some people, the myoclonus can interfere with coordination or become moderate enough to necessitate pharmacologic intervention. That said, many affected individuals can compensate with little or no difficulty.

retrieved_docs.title: Paraneoplastic Syndromes -- History and Physical -- Neurological
retrieved_docs.content: Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome: Clinically characterized by uncontrolled rapid eye movement, body jerks, ataxia, hypotonia, irritability, and commonly affects children less than four years. Opsoclonus is the common manifestation in children, whereas ataxia is more prominent in adults.

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Myoclonus is a complex and often perplexing hyperkinetic movement disorder. Myoclonus describes an involuntary and uncontrollable muscle contraction disorder consisting of sudden, brief, and lightning-like movements of a specific muscle, group of muscles, or even the entire body. As such, myoclonus is a clinical sign and not a diagnosis in and of itself.

retrieved_docs.title: Tilt Table -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The tilt table test is a safe procedure. However, myocardial infarction or stroke may develop in persons with significant coronary or carotid artery stenosis. This is very rare. Arrhythmic complications may be represented by prolonged bradycardia, which may be treated with atropine. Ventricular fibrillation is extremely rare. Some patients may vomit after tilt testing or have severe weakness lasting for several hours. A headache is common, especially after nitroglycerine use. A few seconds of extremity myoclonus is common after syncope is noted before returning to consciousness (convulsive syncope). This should not be confused by the providers for seizures. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1574
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics are linked to خلل الحركة المتأخر, a late‑onset movement disorder (commonly known as tardive dyskinesia) that can develop after prolonged use of these medications; recognizing this relationship is essential for clinicians to monitor patients on antipsychotic therapy for abnormal involuntary movements and to adjust treatment strategies to reduce the risk of this adverse effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 192
retrieved_docs.content: interrelated entities demonstrate how drug‑induced, medically‑caused, neurodegenerative, and age‑related psychiatric conditions intersect through shared diagnostic categories, overlapping symptom clusters (anxiety, mood disturbance, psychosis, confusional states, motor stereotypies, affective blunting), and common neurobiological mechanisms such as brain atrophy and cholinergic dysfunction, underscoring the importance of integrated assessment and management strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 681
retrieved_docs.content: Behavioral/psychiatric manifestations are described as a subcategory of الهَذَيان, and الهَذَيان itself is classified within متلازمة مُضَادّات الفِعْلِ الكولينيّ (SMQ). In addition, حالة ارتباكية دون الحادة and متلازمة الهلوسة العضوية are also identified as members of متلازمة مُضَادّات الفِعْلِ الكولينيّ (SMQ). Together, these entities illustrate that the anticholinergic syndrome (SMQ) encompasses a range of related conditions, including الهَذَيان, a non‑acute confusional state, and an organic hallucinosis syndrome, all of which can present with behavioral and psychiatric features.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1982
retrieved_docs.content: Constipation (إِمْساك) is clinically associated with vomiting (تَقَيُّء), indicating that patients who experience difficulty passing stool may also present with episodes of emesis, reflecting a possible shared gastrointestinal dysfunction or underlying condition that links these two symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Transitory is described as a temporary medical condition whose clinical course is characterized by غشي, indicating that episodes of drowsiness or altered consciousness are a typical feature of its progression. This relationship underscores that patients experiencing a transitory condition may present with غشي as part of the condition’s natural course.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: The anticholinergic syndrome (متلازمة مُضَادّات الفِعْلِ الكولينيّ (SMQ)) is a broad clinical entity that encompasses a variety of neuro‑psychiatric and autonomic manifestations. Within this syndrome, the construct “الهَذَيان” is a member and serves as the parent of “Behavioral/psychiatric manifestations,” indicating that behavioral and psychiatric symptoms are considered part of the anticholinergic effect profile. Additional components classified as members of the SMQ include “تسرع القلب الارتدادي” (rebound tachycardia), “حالة ارتباكية دون الحادة” (mild confusional state), and “متلازمة الهلوسة العضوية” (organic hallucination syndrome). The category “تأثيرات الانسحاب والرجوع” (withdrawal and rebound effects) has “تسرع القلب الارتدادي” as a child, linking withdrawal phenomena directly to the occurrence of rebound tachycardia within the anticholinergic framework. Separately, the diagnostic grouping “Senility and organic mental disorders” is classified under multiple overlapping

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1232
retrieved_docs.content: Non‑infectious encephalitis (SMQ) encompasses a range of neurobehavioral and motor disturbances. It includes اضطراب التصرف (behavior disorder) as a component, as well as both الاضطراب العابر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو المحافظة عليه (transient disorder in awakening onset or maintenance) and الاضطراب المستمر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو استمراره (persistent disorder in awakening onset or continuation). Additionally, فرط النشاط الحركي (hyperkinetic activity) and فرط النشاط العضلي (muscle hyperactivity) are clinical manifestations of SMQ. Together, these interconnected features characterize the complex presentation of التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ) and are essential for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 957
retrieved_docs.content: Non‑infectious encephalitis (SMQ) encompasses both a transient disorder in the initiation or maintenance of awakening and a persistent disorder in the initiation or continuation of awakening, each of which is considered a member of the broader non‑infectious encephalitis category. In turn, non‑infectious encephalitis (SMQ) itself is classified as a form of hyperkinetic activity and as a form of muscle hyperactivity. This hierarchical organization links specific awakening‑related disturbances to the overall condition of non‑infectious encephalitis and situates the encephalitis within the larger domains of excessive motor activity and excessive muscular activity, underscoring the relevance of motor and muscular hyperactivity in the clinical understanding and management of these encephalitic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2015
retrieved_docs.content: Coma, stupor, and brain damage are clinical manifestations that frequently co‑occur with عَوَز الأكسجين, indicating that insufficient oxygen supply to the brain can lead to these severe neurological states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: The interpretation of “pohyb,” which means movement, is identified as “باركنسونية ثانوية,” or secondary parkinsonism, indicating that abnormal movement patterns are understood in the context of a secondary parkinsonian syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 0 -- Community 2
retrieved_docs.content: “non‑infectious encephalitis” SMQ, connecting sleep‑wake dysregulation to inflammatory brain processes. Apathy belongs to the “non‑infectious delirium/brain disease” SMQ, which additionally includes attention‑deficit, concentration weakness and early daytime sleepiness, linking cognitive‑motor deficits with delirium‑type brain disease. Gender identity is associated with stereotyped repetitive movements; together with emotional blunting, these signs are interpreted as clinical expressions of localized brain atrophy. Miscellaneous mental‑health disorders are broadly related to an unspecified transient organic mental disorder and are primarily mapped from organic mood disorder, indicating an organic substrate for diverse psychiatric presentations. A confusional state without acute features and an organic hallucination syndrome are members of the “anticholinergic syndrome” SMQ, reflecting anticholinergic toxicity as a cause of acute psychotic‑like states. Schizophrenic mania (“hous

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1162
retrieved_docs.content: The syndrome “الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)” is characterized as a collection of related neuropsychiatric manifestations. It includes “اضطراب التصرف” as one of its constituent features, and “اللامبالاة” is also identified as a member of this syndrome. In addition, the SMQ encompasses deficits in attention (“خلل في الانتباه”), reduced ability to maintain focus (“ضعف التركيز”), and an early presentation of excessive daytime sleepiness (“فرط النوم في النهار الأولي”). Together, these elements define the clinical profile of “الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)”, indicating that patients with this condition may exhibit behavior disorder, apathy, attention and concentration problems, and initial excessive daytime sleepiness as interrelated components of the disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 93
retrieved_docs.content: hallucinations within the same pharmacologically related syndrome. Together, these relationships illustrate how specific organic psychiatric conditions and syndromes are organized under broader diagnostic categories and pharmacological classifications, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and understanding of underlying pathophysiology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2574
retrieved_docs.content: Emotional blunting (تبلد المشاعر) is identified as a clinical manifestation of specific brain atrophy (ضمور المخ المحدد). This relationship indicates that patients who exhibit localized cerebral atrophy may present with reduced emotional responsiveness, and recognizing emotional blunting as a sign of such atrophy can aid clinicians in diagnosing underlying neurological conditions where affective changes are a prominent feature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Other mental conditions are classified as القيء نفسي المنشأ, a term that denotes psychogenic vomiting—a condition in which vomiting arises from psychological factors rather than an organic physiological cause. This classification highlights the importance of recognizing psychological origins when evaluating vomiting symptoms, ensuring appropriate mental‑health assessment and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 0 -- Community 21
retrieved_docs.content: brain pathology (“الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي SMQ”), shares cognitive‑arousal deficits such as attention disorder, concentration weakness and primary excessive daytime sleepiness, underscoring overlap between these non‑infectious brain disorders. Focal brain atrophy (“ضمور المخ المحدد”) produces motor and affective sequelae, manifesting as repetitive stereotyped movements and emotional blunting. Organic psychosis (“ذُهان عضوي”) is linked to schizophrenic delusion (“هوس شَبَقِيّ”), indicating that organic cerebral disturbances can generate psychotic features similar to primary schizophrenia. Withdrawal and rebound effects (“تأثيرات الانسحاب والرجوع”) may lead to rebound tachycardia (“تسرع القلب الارتدادي”) as a specific cardiovascular manifestation of the rebound phenomenon.
